title: Power
summary: How fast energy moves. Use P = W/Δt and P = F·v on cars and stair sprints.

steps.title: How fast you move energy
steps.body:
Two movers carry the same stack of boxes up the same stairs. One takes 1 minute, the other takes 10. They did the **same work** — but the fast one was far more **powerful**.

**Power** is the rate at which energy is transferred.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
**W** is the work done (J), **Δt** is the time it took (s). Power is measured in **watts (W)**, where 1 watt = 1 joule per second.

Set the work and the time below — watch power shoot up as you shrink the time. Same job, done faster, means more power.

steps.prompt: You do a fixed **W = 120 J** of work. Drag the **time** you take and watch your power output.
steps.task: Drag the time down to **half** — from 4 s to 2 s. Then read the two bars.
steps.solution: Power is work over time: P = W / Δt. The work stays 120 J, so cutting the time from 4 s to 2 s halves the denominator — which doubles the power, from 30 W to **60 W**.

steps.title: Power and speed
steps.body:
There's a second face of power. If a force F pushes something moving at speed v, the work piles up as F·d while the distance grows as d = v·Δt. Divide and the time cancels:

P = W/Δt = F·d/Δt = F·v.

So for steady motion against a steady force, power is simply force times speed.

steps.prompt: A car cruises at a **constant 30 m/s** down the highway. Air resistance and friction together push back with **900 N**. What power must the engine deliver to hold that speed?
hints: Constant speed means the engine force just balances the 900 N of drag. Use P = F·v.
steps.solution:
At constant speed the engine's drive force equals the 900 N of resistance, so use P = F·v.

P = 900 × 30 = **27,000 W** (27 kW, about 36 horsepower).

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **65 kg** athlete sprints up a staircase that rises **4.5 m** vertically, taking **5.0 s** at a steady pace. (g = 9.8)

**Part A —** How much work does the athlete do against gravity?
hints: At a steady pace there's no change in KE, so all the work raises gravitational PE: W = mgh.
steps.solution:
Running at a steady pace, the work goes into lifting the body: W = mgh.

W = 65 × 9.8 × 4.5 ≈ **2867 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** What is the athlete's power output on the climb?
hints: Power is the work done divided by the time it took.
steps.solution:
Power is work over time: P = W / Δt.

P = 2866.5 / 5.0 ≈ **573 W** — well above a steady human output, which is why a hard stair sprint is exhausting.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You connected work, time, and power, used P = F·v for steady motion, and measured a real athlete's power climbing stairs.
